questions.text: Choose the correct answer:
questions.subject: English

options.text: Since he came, we had happily married.
options.explanation: Option A is incorrect because the second part of the sentence uses the past perfect tense ("had happily married"), which is not suitable for the context of the sentence.

options.text: Since he had came, we’re happily married.
options.explanation: Option B is incorrect because it uses the past perfect tense ("had came"), which is grammatically incorrect. The correct form is "had come" (had + past participle).

options.text: Since he came, we’ve been happily married.
options.explanation: So, Option C is the correct answer because it uses the present perfect tense ("we've been happily married"), which is appropriate for the context of the sentence.

options.text: Since he came, we’re happily married.
options.explanation: Option D is incorrect because it uses the present tense ("we're happily married"), which does not convey the sense that the event started in the past and is still continuing in the present.

questions.explanation: The given sentence talks about an event that started in the past and is still continuing in the present. This requires the present perfect tense to be used, which is formed by using "have/has" + past participle.

questions.text: Complete the sentences by choosing the most appropriate option, from the given lettered choices (A to D/E) below:He would like _ have a job in the same office as his brother.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: “Too” is an adverb of degree that can mean “excessively,” “also,” or “very.” It is used before an adjective or adverb or before “much”, “many”, “few” etc. It is used when the speaker wants to show so much of a particular quality that something is not possible (Example: I was too excited to sleep.) or when the speaker wants to express that something is more than necessary or acceptable (Example: You’re driving too fast.). It is also used after mentioning an additional person, thing, or fact to show that they are also included in what you are saying (Example: Sana’s got a lovely voice and she’s a good dancer too.).
“To” is a preposition. It can be used to indicate a goal or a direction of movement, as well as a place of arrival. Thus, choosing “to” to complete the given sentence would be most appropriate.

“Of” and “to” are two separate prepositions that can’t be used as a prepositional phrase because it only possible to use two prepositions together if the second one is creating a noun phrase. Hence, “of to” does not make sense grammatically.

The verb “get” is an irregular verb. Its three parts are get, got, gotten. It is used as get + object or get + indirect object + direct object when the speaker wants to express the meaning of “fetch” or “receive” or “obtain” (Example: Can you get me a glass of water?). It is also used as a get + adjective to describe a change of state or situation (Example: Let’s hope she gets better soon.).

questions.explanation: The correct preposition to use over here will be "to" because all others don't make sense here grammatically. Too is used to imply collectiveness, which is not the context here. Of to and get don't make any sense when plugged in.

questions.text: Select the option which fits appropriately in the blank:The teacher set some homework_ the end of the lesson.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "about" is too vague and does not directly specify the timing of when the homework was assigned.
"in" suggests the homework was assigned during the end of the lesson, but doesn't pinpoint the exact timing.
"of" implies the homework was part of the end of the lesson, but doesn't clearly state it was assigned at that time.
"d. at" is correct because it clearly indicates the homework was assigned at the end of the lesson, specifying the exact timing of when the homework was set.

questions.explanation: Prepositions are words that show the relationship between elements in a sentence. Option D is the most appropriate as it correctly connects the subject and the object of the sentence with the relationship of time, which the phrase implies.

questions.text: Select the option which fits appropriately in the blank:What is the cause _ the problem?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "a. of" is the best choice as it precisely identifies the cause as the origin or source that led to the problem occurring.
"on" suggests the cause is acting upon or affecting the problem, but does not clearly state the cause is the source of the problem.
"about" implies the cause is related to or concerning the problem, but does not specify the cause as the root source.
"in" indicates the cause is contained within or part of the problem, rather than being the originating source of the problem.

questions.explanation: A preposition is usually located directly before the word or phrase that it relates to. Option A is the most appropriate preposition as it links the object and the subject of the sentence best.

questions.text: Demonstrate correct use of articles and prepositionsMy friend has been living _ Karachi for two years.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The preposition "on" is used to indicate a surface or a position that is physically above or attached to something.
The preposition "at" is used to indicate a specific location or point in time, but it does not convey the meaning of living in a place.
The preposition "in" is used to indicate the location of a place where someone lives, example: Karachi
The preposition "across" is used to indicate movement from one side of something to the other

questions.explanation: "In" is a preposition used when something is enclosed or located within a defined space, e.g., Karachi. The preposition "on" is used to indicate a surface or a position that is physically above or attached to something.The preposition "at" is used to indicate a specific location or point in time, but it does not convey the meaning of living in a place.The preposition "in" is used to indicate the location of a place where someone lives, for example, Karachi The preposition "across" is used to indicate movement from one side of something to the other.

options.explanation: A and D are incorrect as the sentence already has a main verb. In a single clause we use 1 main verb. If a sentence has multiple clauses, each clause has its own main verb.

C is incorrect because the positioning of the adverb and preposition is wrong. Prepositions are written after adverbs.
A and D are incorrect as the sentence already has a main verb. In a single clause we use 1 main verb. If a sentence has multiple clauses, each clause has its own main verb.

questions.text: The dog sprang _ him.

options.explanation: "On," could also be used to indicate the dog's movement towards him, but "upon" is a more appropriate and commonly used preposition in this context.
The sentence "The dog sprang upon him" suggests that the dog leaped or jumped aggressively towards him. The preposition "upon" is used to indicate a sudden or forceful action in this context.
"In," does not accurately convey the action of the dog leaping towards him."In," does not accurately convey the action of the dog leaping towards him.
"Over," suggests that the dog jumped over him rather than directly towards him.

questions.explanation: The sentence "The dog sprang upon him" suggests that the dog leaped or jumped aggressively towards him. The preposition "upon" is used to indicate a sudden or forceful action in this context.

questions.text: Demonstrate the correct use of articles and prepositions:Danial is playing tennis _ Sunday.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The preposition "in" is generally used to refer to a larger time frame or a period rather than a specific day.

The preposition "on" is used to indicate a specific day or date when an event or activity is taking place. In this case, "On" is used before "Sunday" to specify the day on which Danial is playing tennis.
In this context, we typically use the preposition "on" instead of "at" to indicate the specific day when an event or activity takes place.
The preposition "of" is not typically used to indicate the specific day on which an event or activity takes place.

questions.explanation: The preposition "on" is used to indicate a specific day or date when an event or activity is taking place. In this case, "On" is used before "Sunday" to specify the day on which Danial is playing tennis.

questions.text: You have to wait. He'll be with you _ a minute.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: a) In:The correct option in this context is "in." The sentence would be: "You have to wait. He'll be with you in a minute." This usage indicates that he will be available after the passage of one minute.
b) On:This option is not correct in this context. "On" is not used to indicate a specific amount of time.
c) At:This option is not correct in this context. "At" is not used to indicate a specific amount of time.
d) Of:This option is not correct in this context. "Of" is not used to indicate a specific amount of time.

questions.explanation: The phrase "He'll be with you in a minute" is an expression commonly used to indicate that someone will be available or present to assist you shortly. In this context, the word "in" is used to denote a specific duration of time, which is one minute. So, it means that after waiting for approximately one minute, the person you're referring to will be available to help or speak with you

questions.text: Complete the following sentence: _ I am so lonely here.

options.explanation: "Oh!" is an expression of surprise or exclamation, which does not convey the melancholic tone implied in the sentence.

"alas!" is an appropriate interjection that signals a feeling of regret, sorrow or disappointment, which aligns with the sentiment of being "so lonely" expressed in the rest of the sentence.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with the correct option:You should stick _ your promise.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "You should stick by your promise" is not the correct option because the phrase "stick by" means to support or remain loyal to someone, rather than staying true to a promise, but it has a slightly different meaning. It means to support or stand by someone, rather than keeping a promise. For example, "I will stick by you no matter what."
"You should stick to your promise" is the correct option. This phrase means to remain committed to or follow through on a promise.

"You should stick with your promise" is not the correct option because the phrase "stick with" is typically used to mean staying loyal to someone or something, rather than specifically referring to keeping a promise. For example, "I will stick with this company even during tough times."

"You should stick in your promise" is not the correct option because the phrase "stick in" is not an idiomatic expression and does not convey the intended meaning of keeping a promise. It does not fit in the context of the sentence.

questions.explanation: "You should stick to your promise" is the correct option. This phrase means to remain committed to or follow through on a promise.

questions.text: Complete the sentence by choosing the most appropriate word, from the given lettered choices (A to E) below each. The injured player was taken _ the field.

options.explanation: The word “of'' is a preposition. It is used to show possession, belonging, or origin.
The word “off” can be an adverb, meaning away from a place. As an injured player would be moved away from the field. Thus, choosing “off” to complete the given sentence would be most appropriate.
The word “out” can be an adverb, meaning moving away from the inside of a particular place, especially one that is enclosed or hidden.
The word “in” can be an adverb, used to express a movement with the result that someone or something becomes enclosed or surrounded by something else.
The word “by” can be an adverb, meaning to go past.

questions.explanation: “OFF” means: “away from the place in question; to or at a distance.”, which is our answer.

Option C is not correct because ‘over’ is a preposition used to indicate the position extending directly upwards.
Option D is not correct because ‘in’ is used to indicate the location or position within or inside something.

questions.explanation: The word “off” should be used here as it is a verb, antonym of “On/Open.”

questions.text: Complete the sentences by choosing the most appropriate option, from the given lettered choices (A to D) below each.The doctor is going _ vaccinate me tomorrow.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Of" does not convey the intended meaning in thiscontext. "Of" typically indicates possession or belonging, and usingit in this sentence would imply that the doctor somehow owns or possesses theact of vaccination, which is not the intended meaning.

In this specific sentence, "to" is the correctpreposition to use with the verb "vaccinate" because it indicates thedirection or purpose of the action. The verb "vaccinate" typicallytakes the preposition "to" to show that someone is receiving thevaccination. Therefore, "to" is the most appropriatepreposition in this context to indicate that the doctor will be administeringthe vaccination to you.

"On" generally refers to something beingphysically on top of or touching something else. Using "on" in thissentence would imply that the doctor is physically placed or positioned on you,rather than performing the act of vaccination.

"As" is used to indicate the role or capacity inwhich someone is performing an action. For example, "The doctor acted as amentor." However, in the given sentence, "as" doesn't convey theappropriate meaning because it doesn't indicate the action of vaccinationitself.

questions.explanation: The preposition "OF" is mostly used either in the possession sense, like "he is a part OF that team", or "the painting OF Ahmed" and to relate two parts of a sentence with one another, for example, "a story OF passion", "the city OF Karachi", "Pakistani Heritage" or "Second of January" The preposition "TO" is mostly used as an extension of a verb or action, like "I will be going TO the park", "I want TO play" or "I hope TO see you again" The preposition "ON" is used as an indication of place, or of time, like "I am ON the plane", " I will play that song ON the guitar" (guitar is a place in this sense), "What's on TV" or "It on Monday"(in the time sense) The preposition "as" is mostly used to refer to the purpose or role of a noun, like “I work AS a waiter”, or “The pile of books served AS a laptop stand”. As can also be used to tell two things happening at the same time, for e.g “He arrived AS I left”, or as a replacement of because for e.g “He was jealous AS (because) I came first” As the sentence is talking about an action that will happen, the best-suited preposition will then have to be ‘to’ so option B is correct

questions.text: Complete the sentences by choosing the most appropriate option, from the given lettered choices. Two airplanes crashed _ the fog yesterday?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "On" would imply that the airplanes crashed on top of the fog, which doesn't make sense in this context.
"Of" suggests a possession or belonging relationship, which is not relevant to describe the crash in relation to the fog.
When we say that two airplanes crashed "in" the fog, we are describing the location or environment where the crash occurred.
This doesn't make sense in the context

questions.explanation: The most appropriate choice to complete the sentence is "in." When we say that two airplanes crashed "in" the fog, we are describing the location or environment where the crash occurred. In this case, the fog is acting as a descriptive element, indicating that the crash took place within the foggy conditions. Using "on" or "of" would not convey the same meaning. "On" would imply that the airplanes crashed on top of the fog, which doesn't make sense in this context. "Of" suggests a possession or belonging relationship, which is not relevant to describe the crash in relation to the fog. Therefore, "in" is the most suitable choice in this sentence.

Of is a preposition that indicates relationships between other words, such as belonging, things made of other things, things that contain other things, or a point of reckoning.

questions.explanation: The word 'an' is not a preposition. It's not used to start prepositional phrases. 'An' is actually a special kind of adjective called an 'article'.It is an indefinite article.

questions.explanation: 'To' is a preposition and a versatile little word that can be used to say many things. You can use it to indicate a goal or a direction of movement, as well as a place of arrival.

questions.text: Select the correct option:He was killed _ a robber _ a hatchet.

questions.text: Complete the sentence using the most suitable preposition.I shall visit you _ Summers.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The preposition "at" is used for specific times of the day or night, such as "I shall visit you at noon".
The preposition "on" is used for specific days or dates, such as "I shall visit you on Monday".
When discussing visiting someone during a particular season, we use the preposition "in". For example, "I shall visit you in summer".
The preposition "over" is used for a period covering more than one day or occasion, such as "I shall visit you over the weekend".

questions.explanation: When discussing visiting someone during a particular season, we use the preposition "in". For example, "I shall visit you in summer". The preposition "at" is used for specific times of the day or night, such as "I shall visit you at noon". The preposition "on" is used for specific days or dates, such as "I shall visit you on Monday". The preposition "over" is used for more than one day or occasion, such as "I shall visit you over the weekend".

questions.text: Complete the sentences by choosing the most appropriate option, from the given lettered choices (A to D) below each:Her hair was hanging _ her back.

options.explanation: The verb “paint” means to cover the surface (of something) with paint (noun). It is used as the action verb when the subject is covering the surface (of something) with paint in the present.

The verb “painted” is the past tense and the past particle of the verb “paint”. It is used as the action when the subject covered the surface (of something) with paint in the past, an action that is finished and completed in active voice.
The verb phrase “has painted” is used in present perfect tense, for an action that the subject started at some time in the past and is still continuing now.

The verb phrase “was painted” is the past simple passive. Passive voice is used to describe past events, focusing on the object of a verb to emphasize who or what receives an action instead of the subject (the person or thing) that did the action who/which is unknown, unimportant or not the focus of our interest. The verb phrase “was painted” is used to describe that the object got covered with paint. Thus, choosing “was painted” to complete the given sentence would be the most appropriate.

questions.explanation: The key to solving these questions is reading them in your head, filling in each option and then deciphering which option fits in well. For any sentence which refers to something hanging, down is the most suitable preposition.

If the word at isn't working as a connector, then it will most likely be working as an adverb.

questions.text: Choose the BEST Option.That is just an example of what I complain _.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Of is a preposition that indicates relationships between other words, such as belonging, things made of other things, things that contain other things, or a point of reckoning. The object of "of" must be a problem of some kind: you could complain of a headache, an unreliable web server, an unfaithful spouse, mistreatment by police, etc.
'Off' is usually used as an adverb or a preposition. In both cases, it indicates separation or disconnection. For example: She got off the bus at the next stop.
We can use to as a preposition to indicate a destination or direction: We're going to Liverpool next week. Does he want to come to the park with us?
With means 'in the same place as someone or something' or 'accompanying': I don't like tea with milk. There was a free phone with the car.

questions.explanation: Of is a preposition that indicates relationships between other words, such as belonging, things made of other things, things that contain other things, or a point of reckoning. options.explanation: Through can be used as a preposition, an adverb, and an adjective. It has several meanings, including "from one side to the other," "from beginning to end," and "during an entire period": He rode his bicycle through the door and onto the street. You have to read the book through if you want to be prepared for class.
By means ‘as a means of’ something or someone. This preposition often comes in a passive sentence.Example:Do you travel by bus/train/plane/bike? He was undone by me. The letter was written by him.

In (to indicate a location)- I am in my friend’s place now.In (used while doing something) — The tagline should be catchy in marketing a product.In (to indicate opinion, belief, feeling, etc.)- I believe in hardwork.In (specify day, month, season, year) — I prefer to do Maths in the morning.The new academic session will commence in March.In (to indicate colour, shape and size) — This dress comes in four sizes.

questions.explanation: Through can be used as a preposition, an adverb, and an adjective. It has several meanings, including “from one side to the other,” “from beginning to end,” and “during an entire period”: He rode his bicycle through the door and onto the street. You have to read the book through if you want to be prepared for class.

questions.text: My brother's birthday is _ the 5th of November.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: ‘At’ is used for precise times. e.g. At 10:30 am, at night, at dawn, etc.
‘In’ is used for months, years, decades, centuries, and long periods. e.g. In 1991, In December, etc.
‘On’ is used for days and dates. e.g. On the 10th of January. Hence, C is the correct option.
About means ‘on the subject of’ or ‘concerning’ of something/someone. It also means ‘approximately.'e.g. He cares about his classes.

questions.explanation: OPTION A: ‘At’ is used for precise times. e.g. At 10:30 am, at night, at dawn, etc. OPTION B: ‘In’ is used for months, years, decades, centuries, and long periods. e.g. In 1991, In December, etc. OPTION C: ‘On’ is used for days and dates. e.g. On the 10th of January. Hence, C is the correct option. OPTION D: About means ‘on the subject of’ or ‘concerning’ of something/someone. It also means ‘approximately.' e.g. He cares about his classes.

questions.text: Your pen is _ the box.Demonstrate control of tenses and sentence structure .
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Inside means that within the box . So when we want to to specifically identify the position we use inside instead of in

Into" and "inside" are both prepositions that describe location or movement, but they are used in slightly different ways."Into" is used to describe movement or action towards the interior of something. For example, "She walked into the room," means that she moved from outside the room and into its interior."On the other hand, "inside" describes the location of something within the interior of another object or place. For example, "The keys are inside the drawer" means that the keys are located within the interior of the drawer.
this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: The word inside implies that the thing is physically enclosed – it is in a container (a box, a vehicle, a building with walls, etc.).This means that when talking about location, time, being included, or other situations where you are not physically surrounded, you should use in, not inside.

questions.text: Complete the sentence: The train leaves _ 5 o'clock, hurry up!

options.explanation: "At" refers to a specific time, place or position. In this case, as time is mentioned "At" is most suitable.
"In" refers to a general, longer period of time, such as months, decades, or centuries and is used to describe a general space.
"Around" is often used with verbs of movement.
"Under" describes in or into a position below or beneath something.

This doesn't properly complete the sentence, as "to getting a high score" doesn't convey the right meaning.

questions.explanation: “In” is used in space and in this case the test. "On" would be used to indicate position. "At" would indicate a specific time or place, and "to" would indicate a goal or direction of movement.

questions.text: We still haven't come _ with an interesting theme for the advertising campaign.

'Up', however, is an adverb used to talk about moving towards a higher position, value, number, or level; hence it is correct.

'down' is incorrect because it doesn't fit with the phrasal verb come up with.' Down' can be used to indicate a direction or a decrease in something, but it doesn't convey the sense of creating or producing something.

'In' is directly related to positions and longer periods of time, which in this case cannot be used. questions.text: The students walk five kilometers to school, so they need to get up very early _ the morning.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: On: The preposition "on" is used to indicate specific days or dates, such as "on Monday" or "on the 15th." However, in this sentence, the emphasis is on the time of day rather than a specific day or date, so "on" is not the appropriate choice.

The preposition "in" can be used to refer to a particular period of time.The sentence "The students walk five kilometres to school, so they need to get up very early in the morning" indicates that the students have to wake up early during the morning hours. The preposition "in" is used to express a specific time or period, such as "in the morning" or "in the afternoon." Option B, "In," is the correct choice to complete the sentence and convey the intended meaning.

Out: The preposition "out" is used to indicate movement away from a location or the state of being outside. In this sentence, there is no indication of the students moving away from the morning or being outside of it, so "out" is not the correct choice.

Of: The preposition "of" is often used to indicate possession or origin. However, it is not the appropriate preposition to use when referring to a specific time period. Therefore, "of" is not the correct choice in this sentence.

questions.explanation: The preposition "in" can be used to refer to a particular period of time.The sentence "The students walk five kilometres to school, so they need to get up very early in the morning" indicates that the students have to wake up early during the morning hours. The preposition "in" is used to express a specific time or period, such as "in the morning" or "in the afternoon." Option B, "In," is the correct choice to complete the sentence and convey the intended meaning.

questions.text: In most countries, children start primary school _ the age of six.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: In: The preposition "in" generally indicates a period of time or a location, but it does not accurately convey the specific age of six in this sentence.
On: The preposition "on" is used to indicate specific days or dates, but it is not suitable for indicating an age like "six."
The preposition "at" is used to refer to arrival at a particular position or achieving a particular state.
To: The preposition "to" typically indicates a direction or goal, but it does not convey the specific age of six in this sentence.

questions.explanation: The preposition "at" is used to refer to arrival at a particular position or achieving a particular state.

questions.text: When she parted _ her parents, her eyes were full of tears.

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: This option is correct as it is a vocabulary test. To part with means to give up possession or control of (something).

questions.text: He got up early _ 8 o'clock _ the morning. Fill the spaces with the most appropriate words.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Incorrect because "on" is used for days and dates, not specific times.
Correct because "at" is used for specific times, and "in" is used for parts of the day.
Incorrect because "on" is not used for parts of the day.
Incorrect because "upon" is not typically used to indicate parts of the day in this context.

questions.explanation: Usually, the preposition that is used to refer to time is ''at''. In some cases, ''by'' may also be used. Additionally, when we do not specify a fixed time and refer to a time period such as morning, evening, afternoon, etc. then we use ''in". Hence, ''He got up early at 8 o'clock in the morning" is the only correct answer here.

questions.text: Complete the following sentence: I will show you the picture _ the palace.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Incorrect because "off" does not make sense in this context; it suggests detachment or removal.
Incorrect because "at" indicates location, not possession or content of a picture.
Correct because "of" indicates that the picture depicts or belongs to the palace.
Incorrect because "in" suggests something inside the palace, not the subject of the picture.

questions.explanation: The preposition of is used to show the relation of one thing to another. Off can be used as an adverb or a proposition. It gives the meaning of removal or disconnection. At expresses the location or, in some cases, the timing of an event. In is the proposition used to show the location of something inside something else.

questions.text: Don’t poke your nose _ my affairs.

options.explanation: When you use “in” you are indicating the position or that something is within another thing.
“On” is used to indicate that something is on top of another.
“Into” we use when something or someone comes from outside to the inside. It shows where something is going hence C is the correct answer.
“By” is used to indicate how something is achieved.

questions.explanation: As it is being suggested that she's interfering poking her nose into the matters.

questions.text: I always liked to lean _ the side of mercy

this option is incorrect.

Correct because "confided in" means to trust someone with private information.
Incorrect because "confided on" is not a correct construction.
Incorrect because "confided of" is not a correct construction.

questions.text: The injured player was taken _ the field.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Off : It means to move away from something or somewhere. Here ‘off’ suits the best. Therefore, option ‘A’ is correct.
Of : It can be used to help quantify a time or measurement (e.g., ‘the fifth of September’ or ‘three kgs of potatoes’) and even identify a location (e.g., ‘south of California’).
Out : It is used to show movement from inside to outside.
In : It is used to indicate location or position within or inside something.

questions.explanation: “Off” is used here as it shows disconnection from a person, place or object, i.e away from someone or something, in this case the field.

questions.text: He made _ a story when he was late for the first class, but the teacher didn't believe him.

"Below" is incorrect as it is not applicable to expressing a timeframe for staying on the runway.
"On" is incorrect because it doesn't indicate a specific duration; it simply mentions the plane being on the runway without specifying the time.

questions.text: The plane wasn't allowed to take _ because of bad weather conditions.

Option B (Up) is incorrect because the correct phrase is "take off," not "take up," to indicate the plane's departure from the ground.
When the plane is leaving the runway, it is said that the plane is taking off
Option D (To) is incorrect because "take to" doesn't convey the meaning of a plane departing due to bad weather conditions. The correct phrase is "take off."

questions.explanation: The plane wasn't allowed to take off because of bad weather conditions. When a plane leaves the runway, it is called taking off.

questions.text: The weather is pleasant here_the spring.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The preposition “above” is used to refer to a position that is at a higher level than (something), or a position in extended space over and not touching.
The preposition “around” is used to refer to a position or direction surrounding (something), along the outside of (something), or from one part of to another (of something).
The preposition “in” is used to refer to periods of time, including seasons, such as spring.
The preposition “on'' is used to refer to a position (of something) above the surface, while physically touching the surface. It is also used to refer to specific days of the week.

questions.explanation: The correct preposition is “in”.

questions.text: It tripped _the cat and fell downstairs.

options.explanation: Over: It is the right option. It refers to a thing that went over the cat and fell downstairs. A is the most suitable option for this. Something can’t trip IN or ON the cat. ONTO is also a wrong option.
On: This preposition suggests that the subject tripped and fell onto the cat.
Onto: This preposition implies that the subject tripped and fell onto the cat, similar to option B.
In: This preposition does not fit the context of tripping and falling, as it suggests being inside something.

questions.explanation: A is the right option. It refers to a thing that went over the cat and fell downstairs. A is the most suitable option for this. Something can’t trip IN or ON the cat. ONTO is also a wrong option.

questions.text: He complains _ headache.

options.explanation: At: This preposition is not typically used to express complaints about physical conditions like a headache.
From: This preposition suggests the source or origin of something, but "complains from headache" is not a common expression.
Of: This is grammatically correct. At, from and to are all wrong. He complains of something which is in this case a headache.
To: This preposition is not suitable for expressing complaints about a headache.

questions.explanation: Option C is grammatically correct. At, from, and to are all wrong. He complains of something which is in this case a headache.

questions.text: Fill in with the appropriate Preposition: She climbed _ the ladder to paint the wall:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The preposition "to" indicates movement or direction toward a specific point or goal. However, in this context, the correct preposition is "up" to convey the upward movement on the ladder.
The preposition "up" indicates movement in an upward direction, which accurately conveys the action of climbing on the ladder. This option is correct.
The preposition "of" indicates possession or association, which is not suitable in this context.
The preposition "on" indicates contact or position upon a surface. However, the correct preposition to describe the climbing action is "up."
The preposition "in" indicates being inside or within something, which is not appropriate in this context.

questions.explanation: The correct use of preposition is : She climbed "up"the ladder to paint the wall, thus option B is the correct answer.

questions.text: Which one has proper use of preposition?

options.text: "If I am lying, the curse of Allah be on me and may I be drowned in some period. May I even be deprived from a decent burial!"
options.explanation: This option includes the preposition "from" in the phrase "deprived from a decent burial." However, the more commonly used preposition in this context is "of," as mentioned in option D. So, option A does not have the proper use of preposition.

options.text: "If I am lying. the curse of Allah be on me and may I be drowned in some period, May I even be deprived at a decent burial!"
options.explanation: This option includes the preposition "at" in the phrase "deprived at a decent burial." However, similar to option A, the more appropriate preposition to use in this context is "of," as mentioned in option D. Therefore, option B does not have the proper use of preposition.

options.explanation: This option does not include a preposition in the phrase "deprived off a decent burial." The correct usage would be "deprived of a decent burial," as mentioned in option D. Therefore, option C does not have the proper use of preposition

options.text: "If I am lying, the curse of Allah be on me and may I be drowned in some period. May I even be deprived of a decent burial!"
options.explanation: This option uses the preposition "of" correctly in the phrase "deprived of a decent burial." The preposition "of" is used to indicate possession or relationship between two things, and in this case, it shows that the person wants to be deprived "of" a decent burial.

questions.explanation: The preposition “of” is used to show possession or belonging. In this sentence, the speaker is saying that they may be deprived of something that they would normally have or deserve, which is a decent burial. The other options use incorrect prepositions or omit them altogether.

questions.text: He placed the book _ the vase on the table.

options.explanation: Correct because "beside" means next to or alongside.
Incorrect because "after" suggests a sequence in time, not a physical location.
Incorrect because "on" would imply the book is on top of the vase, which doesn't fit the context.
Incorrect because "in" suggests placing something inside another object, which doesn't fit the context.

questions.explanation: The correct preposition in this sentence is beside, which means next to something.

questions.text: Saba was sick on the bus.[The underlined prepositional phrase functions as a _ in this sentence:]
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Adjuncts are regular adverbs of time, place, manner, degree, and frequency. They are essential to the meaning of the sentence, however, the phrase, “on the bus” has no clear impact on the sentence and the sentence won’t lose its meaning if we drop the phrase.
This is incorrect. Disjunct expresses the writer’s opinion like “sadly”, “realistically” etc.
This option is incorrect. Conjuncts link two clauses or sentences such as “however” etc.
Adverbials give more information about verbs and even if dropped, it won’t have a significant impact on the sentence hence D is the right answer.

questions.explanation: Adverbials give more information about verbs and even if dropped, it won’t have a significant impact on the sentence hence D is the right answer.

questions.text: I prefer fruits _ sweets.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Then: "Then" is an adverb indicating a sequence of events or time. It doesn't fit in the sentence and doesn't make sense when comparing fruits to sweets.
On: "On" is a preposition typically used to indicate a position or location of something. It doesn't fit logically in the sentence when comparing fruits to sweets.
While making preferences, we use the word ‘over’ to make a comparative statement between two or more things. Here, we are comparing fruits and sweets and we prefer fruits over the sweets.
From: "From" is a preposition used to show the point of origin or starting point. It doesn't fit in the sentence when expressing a preference between fruits and sweets.

questions.explanation: We use prefer to say we like one thing or activity more than another. Over is used when there are two clear choices in the phrase. Think of over as setting a list of preferences and putting one over top the other. I prefer jogging over running and walking or as in this case, I prefer fruits over sweets.

questions.text: The doctor is going _ vaccinate me tomorrow.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Going To : It refers to something that is about to happen in the near future. Here ‘go(going)’ is the verb and the options given are prepositions. With every preposition, a phrasal verb gives a different meaning.
Going On: To continue. For example, that party has been going on all night.
Going At: To fight or argue over it.
Going With: To give one's consent or agreement to a person’s actions.

questions.explanation: The expression, "going to", followed by a verb in the infinitive, allows us to express an idea in the near future as in this case the doctor is going to vaccinate me tomorrow.

questions.text: Are the boys still swimming _ the pool?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Is incorrect as Into" is a preposition in English grammar that is used to indicate movement or transformation from one state to another.Into" can also be used to indicate a change in status or condition, as in "She was turned into a frog." It can also be used to indicate an active involvement or interest in something, as in "He's really into baseball."

Is correct, as "in" is a preposition in English grammar that is used to indicate location or position inside something "In" can also be used to indicate a specific time period, as in "I will be back in an hour." It can also be used to indicate inclusion, as in "There are three eggs in the basket.

Is incorrect as "On" is a preposition in English grammar that can be used to indicate a variety of relationships between nouns, such as position, location, time, and manner."On" can also be used to indicate a topic or subject, as in "I read an article on the benefits of exercise." It can be used to indicate a state or condition, as in "He is on the phone.

It iss incorrect, as "At" is a preposition in English grammar that can be used to indicate a variety of relationships between nouns, such as time, location, and manner."At" can also be used to indicate an event or occasion, as in "We will celebrate Christmas at my grandparents' house." It can be used to indicate a price, as in "The book is available at a discounted price.

questions.explanation: It is correct, as "in" is a preposition in English grammar that is used to indicate location or position inside something "In" can also be used to indicate a specific time period, as in "I will be back in an hour." It can also be used to indicate inclusion, as in "There are three eggs in the basket".

questions.text: We are still a long way from _ home.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Use the indefinite article a or an only with a singular count noun whose specific identity is not known to the reader. Use a before nouns that begin with a consonant sound, and use an before nouns that begin with a vowel sound.
"an" is incorrect because it is used before vowel sounds.
"the" is incorrect because it would imply that there is only one specific way to get home, which is not the case.
The sentence would still be grammatically correct and understandable without an article, and would suggest that the speaker is simply emphasizing the distance from home.

questions.explanation: Use the indefinite article a or an only with a singular count noun whose specific identity is not known to the reader. Use a before nouns that begin with a consonant sound, and use an before nouns that begin with a vowel sound.

questions.text: I woke up _ five o'clock _ the morning.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: a) I woke up for five o'clock in the morning: This option is incorrect. "For" is not the correct preposition to use in this context. We usually use "at" instead of "for" when indicating a specific time.
b) I woke up at five o'clock in the morning: This option is correct. "At" is the correct preposition to use when specifying a particular time.
c) I woke up in five o'clock at the morning: This option is incorrect. "In" is not the correct preposition to use here. Additionally, we use "morning" without the article "the."
d) I woke up at five o'clock for the morning: This option is incorrect. Again, "for" is not the correct preposition to use in this context.

questions.explanation: “At” and “in” are the correct prepositions. The preposition “for” is used to refer to an extended period; a duration (how long something lasts). The preposition “in” is used to refer to periods of time during the day (not specific times), such as the morning. The preposition “at” is used to refer to specific times on the clock or points of time in the day; a precise time, such as five o’clock.

questions.text: In each of the following questions, four alternative sentences are given.Choose the CORRECT option.
questions.subject: English

options.text: The pollution in the city had a bad effect about me.
options.explanation: This option is INCORRECT. The preposition "about" is not the correct choice here. "About" is typically used to indicate the topic or subject of a conversation, as in "I'm talking about the weather." In this sentence, we need a preposition that indicates the impact or influence of the pollution on the speaker.

options.text: The pollution in the city had a bad effect for me
options.explanation: This option is also INCORRECT. The preposition "for" is often used to indicate purpose or benefit, as in "I bought a book for my friend." In this sentence, we need a preposition that indicates the impact or influence of the pollution on the speaker.

options.text: The pollution in the city had a bad effect in me.
options.explanation: This option is INCORRECT again. The preposition "in" is often used to indicate location or containment, as in "The book is in my bag." In this sentence, we need a preposition that indicates the impact or influence of the pollution on the speaker.

options.text: The pollution in the city had a bad effect on me.
options.explanation: This option is CORRECT! The preposition "on" is the correct choice here. "On" indicates the impact or influence of one thing (the pollution) on another thing (the speaker). This sentence suggests that the pollution has had an adverse effect on the speaker's well-being.

questions.explanation: Effect (on somebody/something) : a change that somebody/something causes in somebody/something else; a result.

questions.text: Father must _ left his briefcase at the office.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Of" is a preposition, and it doesn't fit into the blank in the given sentence. Prepositions are used to show relationships between nouns or pronouns and other words in a sentence. In this context, "of" does not make sense and is not the correct option.

"Had" is the past participle form of the verb "have." In this sentence, it could be used in the past perfect tense to indicate an action that was completed before another past action. However, when combined with "must" in the sentence, it creates redundancy and doesn't make grammatical sense. So, "had" is not the correct option here.

"Have" is the base form of the verb, and it can be used in the sentence to create the phrase "must have." This construction is used for speculating about a past action that is likely to have happened. So, the correct option is "Option C: Have." The complete sentence is: "Father must have left his briefcase at the office."
"Opt" is a verb that means to choose or select. However, it does not fit the context of the sentence, and it is not the correct option.

questions.explanation: We use must have + past participle when we are sure of what happened. Hence "must have" is correct. ‘Of’ is used to show ownership or a distance from something or a result coming from something. As the sentence is not in the third person singular, ‘has’ is not used. ‘Are’ is used for plural nouns, which is not the case in this sentence ('Father' is singular). ‘Opt’ is used to make a choice, especially of one thing or possibility, instead of others. In this case, the briefcase is an essential item and is necessary.
